elements.sent-2411-001: Recent large-scale diffusion models generate high-quality images but struggle to learn new, personalized artistic styles, which limits the creation of unique style templates.
elements.sent-2411-002: Fine-tuning with reference images is the most promising approach, but it often blindly utilizes objectives and noise level distributions used for pre-training, leading to suboptimal style alignment.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose the Style-friendly SNR sampler, which aggressively shifts the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) distribution toward higher noise levels during fine-tuning to focus on noise levels where stylistic features emerge.
elements.sent-2411-004: This enables models to better capture unique styles and generate images with higher style alignment.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method allows diffusion models to learn and share new ``style templates", enhancing personalized content creation.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate the ability to generate styles such as personal watercolor paintings, minimal flat cartoons, 3D renderings, multi-panel images, and memes with text, thereby broadening the scope of style-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recently, large-scale text-to-image diffusion models~\cite{ldm,sd3,sdxl,stablecascade,imagen,flux1-dev,ediff have achieved remarkable progress in visual content creation.}
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, open-weights like Stable Diffusion series~\cite{ldm,sd3 and FLUX~\cite{flux1-dev have been among the most notable for their photorealistic image quality and language understanding capabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Behind this strong performance lies the advancement of the diffusion framework that encompasses the principles of score-based models~\cite{sde and flow matching~\cite{flowmatching, rectifiedflow , diffusion formulations~\cite{sde,flowmatching,rectifiedflow,ddpm , loss weighting~\cite{p2,understanding , noise level scheduling~\cite{simple,edm , and architectural improvements~\cite{dit,sd3,edm2 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These advancements have predominantly focused on generating high-quality images with respect to object-centric benchmarks~\cite{t2icompbench,geneval and metrics~\cite{fid,kynkaanniemirole .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Motivated by the success of text-to-image models, there is a growing need for style-driven generation, where the generated samples capture styles desired by individual users or artists.
elements.sent-2411-012: Here, ``style" encompasses various elements such as color schemes, layouts, illumination, and brushwork~\cite{gatys2016image,styledrop,li2017universal,quilting —all contributing to the unique nuances of an image.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, relying solely on text prompts has its limitations in capturing these complex and personal artistic styles, especially those not present in the pre-training data.
elements.sent-2411-014: To enable style-driven generation, researchers have turned to fine-tuning~\cite{dreambooth,styledrop text-to-image models~\cite{imagen,muse using reference images of the desired styles, often employing lightweight adapters such as LoRA~\cite{lora , as this remains the most effective method for accurately capturing and reproducing new artistic styles.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, they blindly apply the same objective functions and noise level distributions used for pre-training—originally optimized for object-centric benchmarks~\cite{geneval,t2icompbench —without considering the unique characteristics inherent to style images, which are distinct from object-centric images.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Consequently, even with numerous style-driven generation studies~\cite{styledrop,dco,ipadapter,rb-modulation,stylealign , we observe prevalent failure cases when fine-tuning diffusion models on style references.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Addressing these failures sometimes requires two-stage training with heavy human feedback~\cite{styledrop .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we address these limitations by introducing the Style-friendly SNR sampler, a method that significantly enhances the model's ability to capture new artistic styles during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our approach is motivated by two key observations 1) diffusion models struggle to learn new styles, and 2) styles emerge at higher noise levels.
elements.sent-2411-020: Building upon these observations, we propose adjusting the noise level sampling in the diffusion model's objective function during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-021: By directly sampling signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) using our Style-friendly SNR sampler, we bias the distribution towards higher noise levels where style features emerge, such as color schemes, layouts, and illumination—key elements for style representation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike previous methods~\cite{sd3,dco , our approach intensifies the focus on capturing these stylistic aspects.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our Style-friendly SNR sampler enables state-of-the-art models such as FLUX-dev~\cite{flux1-dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5~\cite{sd3,sd35-8b to effectively learn style templates with unprecedented accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our method leads to a more faithful style-driven generation, capturing the uniqueness of reference style images.
elements.sent-2411-025: Moreover, we unveil the key components that explain why previous works excel at learning object-centric concepts but struggle with styles, providing deeper insights into the diffusion process for style-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-026: Ultimately, our approach facilitates the creation of style templates from reference images, which can be easily shared and utilized to practitioners for content creation, expanding the capabilities of text-to-image diffusion models.

elements.sent-2411-027: Various diffusion models~\cite{sohl2015deep,ddpm,sde,flowmatching,rectifiedflow are based on the forward process that progressively degrades data $x_{0}$ into pure noise $x_{1}$ as time $t$ progresses from $0$ to $1$, following the unified formulation below: \begin{equation}x_t=\alpha_tx_0+\sigma_t\epsilon, \label{eq:forward}\end{equation} where $\alpha_t$ and $\sigma_t$ are predefined noise schedules, and $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0,I)$ represents standard Gaussian noise.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Recent state-of-the-art diffusion models, such as Stable Diffusion 3 (SD3)~\cite{sd3 and FLUX~\cite{flux1-dev , utilize the noise schedule from rectified flow~\cite{flowmatching,rectifiedflow with $\alpha_t=1-t$ and $\sigma_t=t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: This choice is effective due to straight diffusion trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-030: While the diffusion process is commonly parameterized by the timestep $t$, Kingma et al.~\cite{vdm,understanding characterize the noise level using the log signal-to-noise ratio (log-SNR): \begin{equation}\lambda_t = \log\left(\frac{\alpha_t^2}{\sigma_t^2}\right). \label{eq:logsnr}\end{equation} In the case of rectified flow, $\lambda_t=2\log\left(\frac{1-t {t}\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In our work, we demonstrate that adjusting the timestep distribution based on $\lambda_t$ facilitates learning styles more effectively than using $t$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Kingma et al.~\cite{vdm,understanding showed that the objectives of various diffusion models~\cite{ddpm,ldm,flowmatching,rectifiedflow,edm,ncsn,sde share the same form.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The general loss function is expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{DM}(x_0) = \mathbb{E}_{\lambda\sim p(\lambda)}\bigg[||f_{\theta}(x_\lambda,\lambda)-f(x_\lambda,\lambda)||^2\bigg], \label{eq:loss}\end{equation} where $f(x_\lambda,\lambda)$ is the target function.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the case of rectified flow, the model $f_{\theta}$ predicts the velocity $f(x_\lambda,\lambda)=\epsilon-x_0$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Considering the role of $p(\lambda)$, it serves as a weighting factor in the loss function that determines the importance assigned to different noise levels during training: \begin{equation}L_{DM}(x_0) = \int_{\lambda_{min}}^{\lambda_{max}} p(\lambda)||f_{\theta}(x_\lambda,\lambda)-f(x_\lambda,\lambda)||^2d\lambda \notag = E_{\lambda\sim U(\lambda_{min},\lambda_{max})}\bigg[p(\lambda)||f_{\theta}(x_\lambda,\lambda)-f(x_\lambda,\lambda)||^2\bigg]. \label{eq:p2w}\end{equation} From this perspective, we can observe the inductive bias imposed on the diffusion model through $p(\lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Prior works~\cite{p2,dieleman2024spectral discuss that the model learns low-frequency information at large noise levels (small $\lambda$) and learns high-frequency details at small noise levels (large $\lambda$).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Consequently, diffusion models generate images in a coarse-to-fine manner~\cite{heat,dieleman2024spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Previous diffusion models have predominantly been designed based on the timestep $t$ rather than the log-SNR $\lambda_t$, with $t$ typically sampled from a uniform distribution~\cite{ddpm,ldm,dit during training.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, the importance of each noise level in diffusion loss is determined solely by the function $\lambda_t$.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, EDM~\cite{edm argues that this approach entangles the design space of diffusion models and proposes sampling noise variance $\sigma_t$ from a log-normal distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-041: SD3~\cite{sd3 extends this approach to the training of text-to-image models by sampling logit function of timestep $\log\left(\frac{t {1-t}\right)$ from a normal distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: As mentioned earlier, SD3 follows the rectified flow formulation and has the log-SNR as $\lambda_t=2\log\left(\frac{1-t {t}\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, they propose shifting timestep $t$ to $t_{\text{new }$ by $k$ for high resolution training: \begin{equation}t_{\text{new}} = \frac{k t}{1 + (k - 1) t}, \label{eq:t_shift}\end{equation} which is equivalent to shifting $\lambda_t$ by $-2\log k$ as follows: \begin{equation}\lambda_{t_{\text{new}}} = 2 \log\left(\frac{1 - t_{\text{new}}}{t_{\text{new}}}\right) = \lambda_t - 2 \log k. \label{eq:lambda_shift}\end{equation}.}
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Diffusion Models Struggle to Capture Styles.
elements.sent-2411-044: We begin by examining the fine-tuning capability of a recent state-of-the-art model.
elements.sent-2411-045: Many current text-to-image models concentrate on object-centric image quality and demonstrate their performance.
elements.sent-2411-046: Among the latest diffusion models, FLUX~\cite{flux1-dev , the representative text-to-image model, excels in object-driven image generation through fine-tuning, enabling high-quality generation of fine-tuned objects across various scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In \cref{fig:dreambooth a, the dog-themed backpack integrates well into the nighttime surfing scene, matching the appropriate evening lighting.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The dog below is dressed in new clothes, and the typography is also well-generated.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, FLUX struggles to capture styles in terms of color schemes, illumination, and brushwork when fine-tuning with the SNR sampler used for pre-training~\cite{sd3 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: In \cref{fig:dreambooth b, the ``glowing" example only causes the sloth's fur to glow but does not reflect the dark background and blue lighting of the reference.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Similarly, for the Van Gogh oil painting reference, while it captures the blue color tone, it fails to reproduce Van Gogh's distinctive brushstrokes.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Styles Emerge at Higher Noise Levels.
elements.sent-2411-052: To investigate the failure of fine-tuning styles, we perform further analysis related to styles.
elements.sent-2411-053: We investigate the noise level at which the style features emerge when generating images with text-to-image diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-054: We incorporate style descriptions, such as `in flat cartoon illustration' only at specific intervals during the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-055: As illustrated in \cref{fig:switch , we implement the text-conditioned generation process using text $y_{\text{w/o style }$ to exclude style descriptions during the early denoising steps, and then switch to text $y_{\text{w/ style }$ to include style descriptions in the remaining steps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: When style descriptions are used throughout all denoising steps, the generated images align well with desired styles (\cref{fig:switch a).}
elements.sent-2411-057: Interestingly, omitting style descriptions during just the initial 10\% of the denoising steps results in images where styles are not adequately reflected (\cref{fig:switch c).}
elements.sent-2411-058: These images closely resemble those generated without any style descriptions at all—yielding photorealistic samples (\cref{fig:switch d).}
elements.sent-2411-059: The case that uses $y_{\text{w/o style }$ in the later denoising steps is provided in \cref{fig:switch_reverse .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: This observation indicates that the stylistic features are determined during the early stage of the denoising process, corresponding to an interval where the log-SNR $\lambda_t$ is small (\ie, higher noise levels).
elements.sent-2411-061: The subsequent denoising steps primarily render content and fine details that are independent of styles.
elements.sent-2411-062: Thus, missing the style prompt in the initial steps significantly hinders the model's ability to generate images in desired styles, emphasizing the importance of early-stage conditioning for style representation.

elements.sent-2411-063: Our observations indicate the key motivation for style learning.
elements.sent-2411-064: While styles emerge in the early steps of the denoising process, the current fine-tuning process utilizes an SNR sampler from pre-training which prioritizes object-centric generation~\cite{t2icompbench,geneval as shown in the green line of \cref{fig:prob_logsnr .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: This SNR sampler places greater emphasis on intermediate steps to capture fine details of objects better, yet it does not sufficiently focus on the noise levels where styles emerge.
elements.sent-2411-066: As a result, despite excelling in object-driven generation, the current fine-tuning struggles to fully capture and represent target styles in style-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-067: Building upon this motivation, we propose to fine-tune diffusion models by biasing the noise level distribution towards higher noise levels (lower log-SNR $\lambda_t$ values) where stylistic features emerge.
elements.sent-2411-068: Specifically, we sample log-SNR from a normal distribution: \begin{equation}\lambda_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\text{low}},\sigma^2),\end{equation} with a lowered mean $\mu_{\text{low }$, thereby focusing the training on the critical noise levels essential for style learning.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We set the standard deviation $\sigma = 2$ to maintain a balance between concentrating on the critical noise levels and providing sufficient variation in noise levels for effective learning.
elements.sent-2411-070: While timestep shifting in \cref{eq:t_shift weakly biases the noise level distribution, a Style-friendly SNR sampler allows for a more aggressive emphasis on desired noise levels.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Setting the mean to $\mu = -6$ concentrates timesteps between 0.8 and 1.0, where style features emerge (\cref{sec:observation ).}
elements.sent-2411-072: This enables us to support the diffusion model in fine-tuning with a strong focus on styles across various style templates.

elements.sent-2411-076: To effectively learn the stylistic features encompassing both visual and linguistic characteristics, we train LoRA on the attention layers of both modalities.

elements.sent-2411-178: In this section, we review the baseline methods used for comparison and highlight how our approach differs from them in addressing style-driven generation.
elements.sent-2411-179: The baseline methods include the fine-tuning-based methods—SD3 sampler~\cite{sd3 , Direct Consistency Optimization (DCO)~\cite{dco , and our method—as well as the zero-shot methods IP-Adapter~\cite{ipadapter , RB-Modulation~\cite{rb-modulation , and Style-Aligned~\cite{stylealign .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: All fine-tuning-based methods are trained using the same reference images and training prompts to ensure a fair comparison.
elements.sent-2411-181: Notably, our Style-friendly SNR sampler disables timestep shifting~\cite{sd3 during fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-182: For the fine-tuning process, we utilize LoRA~\cite{lora with a rank of 32 (except the LoRA rank analysis) and a learning rate of $10^{-4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The models are trained with a batch size of 1, employing gradient accumulation over 4 steps.
elements.sent-2411-184: All experiments are implemented using the Hugging Face Diffusers library version 0.31.0 and conducted on a single NVIDIA A40 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-185: The FLUX-dev model is trained with gradient checkpointing to save memory consumption during training.
elements.sent-2411-186: Direct Consistency Optimization (DCO)~\cite{dco is a fine-tuning method inspired by direct preference optimization~\cite{dpo commonly used in large language models (LLMs).}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Instead of directly minimizing the diffusion loss, DCO aims to ensure that the diffusion loss of the fine-tuned model is lower than that of the pre-trained model on the reference data.
elements.sent-2411-188: The objective function is defined as: \begin{equation}L_{DCO}(x_0) = E_{t,\epsilon}\bigg[-log\sigma(-\beta T w(t) \notag ||f_{\theta}(x_t,t)-f(x_t,t)||^2 -||f_{\phi}(x_t,t)-f(x_t,t)||^2)\bigg]. \label{eq:dco}\end{equation} In this objective, the parameter $\beta T$ controls the strength of the preference towards the fine-tuned model over the pre-trained model.
elements.sent-2411-189: DCO increases the relative likelihood of the fine-tuned model over the pre-trained model, penalizing less when the fine-tuned model's loss is smaller.
elements.sent-2411-190: This helps preserve the text-to-image alignment of the pre-trained model.
elements.sent-2411-191: However, DCO requires computations involving both the fine-tuned and pre-trained models, making it computationally more intensive than directly fine-tuning using the standard diffusion loss.
elements.sent-2411-192: In our experiments, we observed that using a large value of $\beta T=1000$ resulted in slower convergence and suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-193: Therefore, we set $\beta T = 1$ to achieve better results.
elements.sent-2411-194: IP-Adapter~\cite{ipadapter is designed to enable text-to-image models to generate identity-preserving images by training a compact adapter that encodes CLIP image embeddings~\cite{clip .}}
elements.sent-2411-195: This adapter introduces the CLIP image embedding as an additional input by concatenating its output with the text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-196: The parameter-efficient nature of IP-Adapter allows for easy training and deployment across various text-to-image models.
elements.sent-2411-197: However, a notable limitation is its restricted style alignment due to the expressive constraints of CLIP embeddings, which may result in generated images that do not fully capture detailed stylistic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-198: RB-Modulation~\cite{rb-modulation is a zero-shot approach using Stable Cascade~\cite{stablecascade , a model accepting both CLIP image embeddings and text embeddings as inputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: During the denoising process, RB-Modulation employs gradient guidance of a CSD, a model fine-tuned from CLIP to measure style similarity, resembling classifier guidance~\cite{guided-diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-200: At each denoising step, CSD computes the similarity between the approximated $x_0$ and the reference image, guiding the generation process to enhance this similarity.
elements.sent-2411-201: RB-Modulation also aggregates multiple attention features.
elements.sent-2411-202: However, this approach relies on models that accept CLIP image embeddings, limiting model selection.
elements.sent-2411-203: Additionally, using gradient guidance of CSD increases inference costs, making the generation process more computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-204: Style-Aligned~\cite{stylealign generates consistent sets of images with the same style by ensuring that features of each image attend to those of a reference image through shared key and value features in self-attention layers of image tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-205: It first maps the reference image to noise using DDIM inversion~\cite{ddim and shares self-attention features during denoising.}
elements.sent-2411-206: The fidelity to the reference style can be controlled by amplifying the self-attention logits in the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, Style-Aligned is not directly applicable to MM-DiT~\cite{sd3 architecture that lacks image-only self-attention layers.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, artificially amplifying self-attention logits can lead to artifacts and lower-quality images due to conflicting attention features.
elements.sent-2411-209: Offset noise~\cite{offset is a method proposed to fine-tune diffusion models for generating monochromatic images.}
elements.sent-2411-210: During the diffusion process, a constant offset noise—identical across all pixel positions—is added to the standard Gaussian noise, scaled by a small factor (e.g., 0.1).
elements.sent-2411-211: This introduces an explicit bias toward monotonic noise patterns, encouraging the model to learn and reproduce solid colors.
elements.sent-2411-212: While offset noise aids in learning monotonous patterns, it can hinder the model's capacity to learn more complex styles.
elements.sent-2411-213: Here, we additionally experiment with incorporating offset noise into our training process in \cref{tab:offset_add .}
elements.sent-2411-214: Offset noise with a scale of 0.1 improves the SD3 sampler's results in DINO and CLIP-I scores, as many reference styles from the StyleDrop paper~\cite{styledrop have monochromatic backgrounds, favoring this trick.}
elements.sent-2411-215: However, it still does not reach the performance of our Style-friendly SNR sampler.
elements.sent-2411-216: Moreover, when we combine our Style-friendly approach with a smaller scale of offset noise (0.01), we observe a slight improvement in the style alignment of FLUX-dev.
elements.sent-2411-217: This quantitative evaluation is based on the monochromatic backgrounds prevalent in the StyleDrop~\cite{styledrop references.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Our qualitative comparisons in \cref{fig:template_comparison show that offset noise struggles with complex references, failing to capture intricate stylistic details.}
elements.sent-2411-219: This indicates that while offset noise can help with simple, uniform styles, it is vulnerable to complex styles.

elements.sent-2411-249: In this section, we provide detailed information about the setup of our user study.
elements.sent-2411-250: Our user study aims to measure human preferences in two key objectives of style-driven image generation: style alignment and text alignment.
elements.sent-2411-251: To assess these preferences, we conduct pairwise comparisons between our method and each baseline for each objective.
elements.sent-2411-252: Participants are shown the reference image, target text prompt, and two generated images (one from each method) and are asked to choose the image that better satisfies the objective.
elements.sent-2411-253: We collect three responses from each of the 150 participants, resulting in a total of 450 responses for each comparison.
elements.sent-2411-254: The full instructions used in our questionnaires are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-256: Given a reference image and two machine-generated images, select which machine-generated output better matches the style of the reference image for each pair.
elements.sent-2411-257: Please focus only on the style including color schemes, layouts, illumination, and brushstrokes.
elements.sent-2411-258: If it’s difficult to determine a preference, please select ``Cannot Determine / Both Equally".
elements.sent-2411-260: Given a reference image and two machine-generated images, select which machine-generated output better matches the target text for each pair.
elements.sent-2411-261: Please focus only on the text, without regard for the reference image.
elements.sent-2411-262: To ensure reproducibility, we provide pseudo-code implementations of Style-friendly SNR samplers in \cref{fig:code_snr and the addition of LoRA~\cite{lora parameters to MM-DiT for training in \cref{fig:code_lora .}}}
elements.figure-2411-023: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \begin{pythoncode} # Inputs: mu, std, B, latent # sample log-SNR logsnr = torch.normal(mean=mu, std=std, size=(B,)) # compute timestep t t = torch.nn.functional.sigmoid(-logsnr / 2).view(B, 1, 1, 1) # sample noise noise = torch.randn_like(latent) # Generate Gaussian noise # diffuse latent noisy_latent = (1.0 - t) * latent + t * noise \end{pythoncode} \end{minipage} \caption{PyTorch implementation of a Style-friendly SNR sampler.} \label{fig:code_snr}

elements.paragraph-2411-015: Object fine-tuning using Style-friendly SNR sampler.
elements.sent-2411-393: We further evaluate whether our proposed Style-friendly SNR sampler—designed specifically for style learning—affects object-driven generation performance.
elements.sent-2411-394: As shown in \cref{fig:mu_object , our Style-friendly SNR sampler and the original SNR sampler produce qualitatively similar results when fine-tuning on object references.}
elements.sent-2411-395: Our approach successfully captures critical details, including shapes, colors, and prominent text or patterns (e.g., on the bowl and can).
elements.sent-2411-396: However, it occasionally omits subtle features, such as the small teeth of the monster toy.
elements.sent-2411-397: These findings support our hypothesis that distinct approaches are required for object-centric and style-driven fine-tuning; our Style-friendly sampler, while slightly suboptimal for object-centric fine-tuning, excels in capturing nuanced style characteristics.

elements.figure-2411-039: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/mu_object.pdf} \caption{Object fine-tuning comparison. We compare our Style-friendly SNR sampler and the standard sampler on object-driven fine-tuning. Both approaches generate similar overall results, though our Style-friendly sampler occasionally misses minor details, such as the small teeth of the monster toy. Nevertheless, the Style-friendly sampler reliably captures the object's overall shape, color, and key details such as text and patterns on the bowl and can. The object names are written at the top of the reference images.} \label{fig:mu_object}
